coredata.dc:description: The use of carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) in intelligent packaging (IntPack) represents a significant advancement in addressing food safety, quality, and shelf-life concerns. CNMs like carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, and carbon dots possess exceptional properties, making them well-suited for improving packaging materials. These NMs can improve barrier properties, mechanical performance, and incorporate intelligent functionalities like sensing, indicating, and controlled release systems. IntPack systems utilizing CNMs can monitor and respond to environmental changes, providing real-time information on food condition, thus enhancing protection and communication throughout the supply chain. Although the benefits are promising, issues like potential toxicity, environmental effects, and regulatory requirements must be tackled to ensure safe and effective implementation. Future research should focus on scalable production methods, multifunctional hybrid systems, and integration with Internet of Things technologies to fully realize the potential of CNMs in food IntPack.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) in intelligent packaging (IntPack) represents a significant advancement in addressing food safety, quality, and shelf-life concerns. CNMs like carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, and carbon dots possess exceptional properties, making them well-suited for improving packaging materials. These NMs can improve barrier properties, mechanical performance, and incorporate intelligent functionalities like sensing, indicating, and controlled release systems. IntPack systems utilizing CNMs can monitor and respond to environmental changes, providing real-time information on food condition, thus enhancing protection and communication throughout the supply chain. Although the benefits are promising, issues like potential toxicity, environmental effects, and regulatory requirements must be tackled to ensure safe and effective implementation. Future research should focus on scalable production methods, multifunctional hybrid systems, and integration with Internet of Things technologies to fully realize the potential of CNMs in food IntPack.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) have emerged as transformative components in the development of next-generation intelligent packaging (IntPack) systems, addressing critical challenges in food safety, quality, and shelf life (SLife) (Vernekar et al., 2021). The unique physicochemical properties of CNMs—such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, fullerenes, and carbon dots (CDs)—make them highly suitable for enhancing packaging materials with advanced functionalities. These materials not only improve the mechanical strength and barrier performance of packaging but also enable intelligent features like real-time sensing, environmental monitoring, and controlled release of active agents (Kumar et al., 2024; Ramezani et al., 2024). The significance of CNMs in food packaging lies in their ability to meet the growing demands for sustainable and efficient packaging solutions. The food industry faces increasing pressure to ensure product safety, extend SLife, and provide consumers with real-time information about food quality (Naik et al., 2024). CNMs offer innovative solutions by integrating intelligent functionalities into packaging systems. For instance, CD-based films can visually indicate food freshness through color changes, while graphene oxide (GO) composites enhance moisture resistance and mechanical durability. Additionally, the incorporation of CNMs into biobased materials aligns with global sustainability goals by reducing reliance on traditional plastics (Singh et al., 2019).

A wide array of CNMs and their composites has been explored for IntPack applications. These include CNTs with exceptional thermal and electrical properties, graphene derivatives that improve barrier characteristics, and fullerenes known for their unique molecular structures. Moreover, emerging forms like carbon nanofibers (CNFs) and nanodiamonds (NDs) are being investigated for their potential to further enhance packaging performance. The integration of these materials with cutting-edge technologies, e.g., printed electronics and Internet of Things (IoT) devices, is expanding the scope of IntPack systems, enabling continuous monitoring throughout the supply chain (Mahapatra and Chandra, 2023; Musa et al., 2025). Intelligent food packaging (IFP) represents a paradigm shift from passive protection to active interaction with packaged goods and their environment. By incorporating CNMs, these systems can detect spoilage indicators, monitor temperature and humidity levels, and even respond dynamically to environmental changes (Agrawal et al., 2013; Mkhari et al., 2025). However, alongside these advancements come challenges related to potential toxicity, environmental impact, and regulatory compliance. Addressing these issues is crucial for the safe and widespread adoption of CNM-based IntPack solutions.

This review aims to synthesize current knowledge on the applications of CNMs in IFP. It will explore the properties and roles of various CNMs, evaluate their integration into IntPack systems, and discuss their contributions to food safety and sustainability. Additionally, it will highlight challenges such as scalability and regulatory hurdles while outlining future research directions to fully realize the potential of CNMs in revolutionizing food packaging technologies.

The CNM class encompasses a wide variety of nanostructured carbon forms, each possessing unique properties and potential applications in IFP (Vobornik et al., 2023). This overview presents the key elements of the most commonly explored CNMs for this purpose, along with specific examples from recent research.

CNTs offer exceptional value in food packaging due to their unique structural properties. The cylindrical configuration of rolled graphene sheets provides remarkable mechanical reinforcement while maintaining lightweight characteristics (Vobornik et al., 2023). Arora and Padua (2010) demonstrated that incorporating CNTs into polylactic acid (PLA) films significantly enhanced oxygen barrier properties, directly addressing one of the primary challenges in food preservation - oxygen-induced spoilage. This breakthrough is particularly valuable for extending the SLife of oxygen-sensitive foods like meats and oils. Bhattacharya's research on polyethylene/CNT nanocomposite films revealed substantial improvements in water vapor barrier performance and tensile strength (Bhattacharya, 2016). These enhancements solve critical packaging challenges by preventing moisture migration that leads to textural degradation, microbial growth, and reduced SLife. The dual functionality of CNTs - simultaneously improving both mechanical durability and barrier properties - makes them particularly valuable when packaging materials must withstand physical stress during transportation while maintaining tight environmental control around the food product (Bhattacharya, 2016).

Graphene and its derivatives offer unique advantages for IntPack due to their 2D structure and exceptional physical properties (Santana et al., 2022; Sundramoorthy et al., 2018). Another research on chitosan/GO nanocomposite films demonstrated significantly reduced water vapor permeability and enhanced oxygen barrier performance, addressing two critical food degradation factors simultaneously (Han Lyn et al., 2019a). This study is significant because it shows how GO can transform natural biopolymers like chitosan into high-performance packaging materials that extend product freshness while maintaining biodegradability. Starch/GO films revealed superior moisture resistance and mechanical strength, representing a breakthrough for starch-based packaging that typically suffers from moisture sensitivity (Ge et al., 2017). This approach enables the use of renewable starch resources in applications previously dominated by petroleum-based polymers. Gelatin/GO nanocomposites demonstrated enhanced mechanical attributes and improved water vapor barrier performance, providing valuable insights into protein-based films that can be used for packaging protein-rich foods with minimal migration concerns (Nor Adilah et al., 2021). The relationship between GO content and performance improvements establishes practical guidelines for commercial development of these materials (Nor Adilah et al., 2021; Oymaci and Altinkaya, 2016). PVA/rGO nanocomposite films showed improved mechanical strength, thermal stability, and barrier characteristics, offering solutions for packaging applications requiring resistance to temperature fluctuations during distribution or cooking processes (Yilmaz Dogan et al., 2022). This research contributes practical knowledge about how rGO can transform conventional materials into multifunctional packaging solutions.

Fullerenes are a distinct form of carbon, characterized by their intricate molecular structure. This structure consists of carbon atoms bonded together in a closed or partially enclosed network, forming fused rings of five to seven carbon atoms. These remarkable molecules can adopt a range of geometries, including hollow spheres, ellipsoids, tubes, and other captivating shapes. The C60 molecule, commonly referred to as buckminsterfullerene, is the most renowned fullerene. This molecule is composed of 60 carbon atoms arranged in a characteristic soccer ball-like configuration (Ekpete and Orie, 2023). Fullerenes have been studied for various uses, but their application in industrial fluid processing is still a new area of research. Recent studies have looked at adding fullerenes to polymer materials to enhance their barrier and mechanical properties (Ghosh et al., 2024). For example, Pereira et al. (2015) reported improved mechanical properties and thermal stability in PE/fullerene nanocomposite films. Similarly, Kausar and Ahmad (2024) demonstrated enhanced barrier properties and mechanical reinforcement in PLA/fullerene nanocomposite films. Functionalization of fullerenes is often necessary to improve their solubility, compatibility, and reactivity for specific applications. Various covalent or non-covalent functionalization techniques, including cycloaddition reactions, hydrogenation, and supramolecular interactions with a range of molecules or polymers, can be employed to achieve this. In summary, fullerenes exhibit exceptional mechanical properties and thermal stability, making them promising candidates for enhancing IFP systems

CDs are a class of nanoparticles that are generally quasi-spherical in shape and exhibit fluorescent properties, with typical dimensions in the range of 2–10 nm. Various methods, including hydrothermal, pyrolysis, or microwave-assisted synthesis, allow for the synthesis of CDs from various carbon-rich precursors, such as biomass, polymers, or small organic molecules (Tejwan et al., 2022). In the context of IFP, CDs have been explored for their potential to function as fluorescent probes for sensing and monitoring applications, and as delivery systems for the controlled administration of active substances, such as antimicrobials or antioxidants (Tejwan et al., 2022). For instance, M. Yan et al. (2023) created a fluorescent sensor utilizing CDs to detect volatile organic compounds (VOCs) related to food decay. They described the fabrication of a CD-based fluorescent probe capable of detecting fluctuations in pH, which may serve as an indicator of food spoilage.

Carbon black consists of nanoparticles produced through pyrolysis at high temperatures (approximately 1300°C), resulting in a disordered structure. This material is characterized by its exceptionally high surface area (>700 m²/g) and excellent electrical conductivity, making it highly versatile. It is widely used as a conductive filler in RFID tags, as a UV protection agent in coatings, and for reinforcing rubber-based packaging materials. However, concerns have been raised regarding its potential toxicity due to the release of nanoparticles during degradation (González-González et al., 2020a).

Activated carbon is a highly porous material featuring micro- and mesopores ranging from 2 to 50 nm. It is typically synthesized from biomass through pyrolysis followed by alkali or acid activation. Its porous structure makes it effective for various applications, e.g., ethylene scavenging in fruit packaging, moisture control, and adsorption of off-flavors. For instance, polypropylene (PP)/activated carbon composites can reduce ethylene gas levels in fruit packaging by up to 70 %, significantly extending the SLife of produce (González-González et al., 2020a).

In addition to the aforementioned CNMs, several other CNMs have been explored for various applications, including IFP. These include CNFs, NDs, and carbon nanoscrolls (CNAs). CNFs are elongated, fibrous nanostructures composed of graphene layers arranged in a stacked or cup-like configuration. These materials have high mechanical strength, thermal stability, and electrical conductivity, allowing them to be used to reinforce polymer matrices and create conductive composites (Ramezani et al., 2023; Swain et al., 2017). Research has examined incorporating CNFs into packaging films to improve mechanical and barrier characteristics. For instance, Gavrilov et al. (2023) reported enhanced TS, YM, and oxygen barrier performance in PP/CNF nanocomposite films. Diamond nanoparticles, typically 2–10 nm in size, demonstrate exceptional mechanical properties, thermal conductivity, and chemical stability, rendering them appealing for a range of applications such as composites, coatings, and biomedical devices (Pan et al., 2023).

As the use of nanostructured materials in food packaging is still an emerging field, recent research has examined their capability to improve the mechanical and barrier properties of packaging materials (Lionetto and Esposito Corcione, 2021). For instance, Zhang and Kang (2018) reported enhanced mechanical strength and gas barrier performance in PE terephthalate (PET)/NDs nanocomposite films. CNAs are seamless, rolled-up graphene sheets with a tubular structure. Due to their distinctive mechanical, electronic, and optical characteristics, these materials show promise for diverse applications such as energy storage, catalysis, and sensing (Bistričić et al., 2015). The utilization of cellulose nanoparticles in food packaging is currently in the initial phases, with a limited body of research available. Nevertheless, their distinctive characteristics indicate promising applications in the development of advanced packaging materials with improved barrier properties, mechanical performance, or sensing functionalities (Saini et al., 2021). Table 1 presents an overview of different types of CNMs, and other specialized forms like CNFs and NDs, particularly highlighting their roles in IFP and other advanced technologies.

Fig. 1a displays the Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) image of graphene flakes aggregated into a film structure, while Fig. 1b shows the amorphous form of low-pressure diamond powder. Fig. 1c and e describe the structural features of detonation NDs particles that have undergone chemical modification with rhodamine and hydroxyl groups, respectively. Both modified NDs exhibit similar nanocrystalline structures and fluorescence properties akin to diamond autofluorescence. Fig. 1f depicts unmodified detonation NDs particles, which lack visible autofluorescence. This observation indicates that introducing reactive groups on the surface is crucial for achieving fluorescent properties in detonation NDs.

The diverse physicochemical properties of CNMs make them ideal candidates for IntPack applications. CNTs excel in enhancing mechanical strength and barrier properties, with studies showing improved oxygen barrier performance and tensile strength in polymer nanocomposites. Graphene derivatives, particularly GO, demonstrate exceptional moisture resistance and oxygen barrier capabilities, as evidenced by reduced water vapor permeability in various polymer films. CDs stand out for their sensing and fluorescence-based indicator properties, enabling the development of probes for detecting volatile organic compounds and pH changes associated with food spoilage. Fullerenes contribute to mechanical reinforcement and thermal stability in packaging materials, while other CNMs like CNFs and NDs show promise in improving barrier characteristics and mechanical performance. Building upon the diverse properties of CNMs discussed above, Section 3 explores their integration into IntPack systems.

IntPack systems integrate active and intelligent components, enabling them to interact with the food product or its surroundings, providing real-time data on the product's condition and enhancing its protection ( Fig. 2). These systems can perform various functions, such as monitoring and indicating changes in temperature, humidity, gas composition, or microbial growth, as well as releasing antimicrobial agents or antioxidants to extend the product's SLife (Puebla-Duarte et al., 2023). These systems collectively enhance food quality monitoring and consumer decision-making across the supply chain.

TTIs monitor and track the cumulative time-temperature exposure of a food product throughout storage and distribution. TTIs provide valuable information about the potential quality and safety of perishable foods, as their SLife is strongly affected by temperature exposure during the supply chain. Different types of TTIs can be categorized according to their operating principles, including diffusion-based, enzymatic, photochromic, or polymer-based indicators. These indicators can be merged into packaging materials labels, providing a visual or electronic indication of the product's temperature history, enabling better quality control and decision-making regarding the product's safety and SLife (Pandian et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2015).

GIs monitor and indicate the presence or concentration of specific gases within the packaging environment. These indicators are particularly useful for vacuum packaging or modified atmosphere packaging (MAP), where the gas composition plays a vital role in preserving the quality and SLife of the food product. GIs can be designed to detect various gases, such as oxygen, CO2, ethylene, or VOCs, which can be indicators of spoilage or ripening processes. The operating principles of GIs can vary, including colorimetric indicators, electrochemical sensors, or optical techniques (Mills, 2009; Mohammadian et al., 2020; Purohit et al., 2022).

FIs and signal changes in food quality or freshness. These indicators provide valuable insights into spoilage or deterioration, enabling consumers and retailers to make informed decisions regarding the consumption or disposal of the food product. FIs can operate based on various principles, such as monitoring changes in pH, detecting VOCs or other volatile metabolites produced during food spoilage or ripening processes, or monitoring microbial growth or the presence of specific microorganisms associated with food spoilage (Ma et al., 2022; Ramezani et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2023).

Several other IntPack systems can also provide valuable information or functionalities for food products, such as: (i) RFID tags to track and trace food products across the supply chain (Li et al., 2023). (ii) Moisture indicators (MIs), which can be a combination of moisture-sensitive dyes or probes with CNMs, particularly CNTs and graphene. These indicators undergo a visible color change or a change in optical properties, e.g., fluorescence or absorbance, in response to changes in moisture or humidity in the packaging (Fei et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024). (iii) Tamper-evident indicators, developed with CNMs based on their optical properties or their ability to form irreversible structural changes upon tampering. These indicators provide visual or electronic indications of any unauthorized opening or tampering of the packaging (Cheng et al., 2023; Manikandan and Min, 2023). (iv) Interactive packaging systems, which incorporate technologies like QR codes, augmented reality (AR), or near-field communication (NFC) to inform consumers about the product, e.g., nutritional information, cooking instructions, or product traceability (Đurđević et al., 2019). CNMs have been explored for developing interactive packaging systems by leveraging their unique optical properties and their capability to form functional coatings or films. These IntPack systems offer numerous opportunities for improving the functionality of IFP, improving food safety, extended SLife, and better communication of product information to consumers and stakeholders throughout the supply chain (Nufer and Brunton Alan Dalton, 2019; Purohit et al., 2019; Siddiqui et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Improving the functionality of intelligent packaging systems with carbon nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: CNTs, graphene, and CDs have emerged as promising materials for advanced packaging systems due to their unique properties and versatile applications. These materials have outstanding electrical and thermal performance, as well as customizable optical and electronic features, making them useful for creating responsive and interactive packaging. This comprehensive section explores the various applications of CNMs in IntPack, focusing on TTIs, GIs, FIs, spoilage indicators, and other innovative functionalities (Liao et al., 2019; S et al., 2018). CNMs enable precise monitoring of food conditions through innovative sensing mechanisms, paving the way for IntPack solutions.

TTIs are important for tracking the cumulative time and temperature history of food products during storage and transport. CNMs have been effectively utilized in TTIs due to their temperature-dependent electrical resistance (Gao et al., 2020). As an example, a color-changing TTI has been developed using GO. As cumulative time-temperature exposure increases, the indicator shifts from purple to red. This change is attributed to the reduction of GO at elevated temperatures, altering its optical properties. This provides an easy-to-interpret visual cue for consumers and retailers about food freshness (Cho et al., 2023). Also, using inkjet printing technology, researchers have created a flexible TTI by depositing CNT ink onto flexible substrates. This sensor can be easily integrated into existing packaging materials and shows excellent sensitivity to temperature changes, capable of detecting temperature abuse events as short as 30 min (Lu et al., 2020). A graphene-based NFC tag has been developed to track and wirelessly transmit the temperature history of a product during its supply chain journey, enabling real-time monitoring of product quality throughout transportation and storage (Sima et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017).

Finally, TTIs incorporating CNTs or graphene into polymer matrices have been developed. These composites exhibit temperature-dependent electrical resistance, allowing for precise monitoring of time-temperature exposure (Xing et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2024). In some studies, CNTs and graphene have been incorporated into polymer matrices to develop conductive polymer composites for TTI applications. The electrical properties of these composite materials can be adjusted by changing the amount and distribution of the conductive NMs within the polymer matrix. The operating principle of these TTIs is based on the temperature-dependent electrical resistance of the conductive polymer composite (Dan and Elias, 2020; Luo et al., 2022). CNMs, particularly CNTs and graphene, have also been explored for developing Thermal Sensor Arrays (TSAs) in TTI applications. These sensor arrays can be fabricated by patterning CNMs onto flexible substrates or incorporating them into polymer matrices. The operating principle of these TSAs relies on the temperature-dependent electrical resistance or thermal conductivity of the CNMs (Huang et al., 2019; Meng et al., 2023; Tajedini et al., 2025).

The approach and modeling of microbial time-temperature integrators seek to determine their thermal kinetic parameters and forecast their behavior under specific thermal profiles. Fig. 3 outlines four essential components of the application approach: experimental design, parameter estimation, and modeling under both isothermal and non-isothermal temperature conditions (Zhang et al., 2016).

GIs monitor the internal atmosphere of packaging, particularly useful in MAP or vacuum packaging. CNMs, such as CNTs and graphene, have been utilized as electrode materials in electrochemical GIs for IntPack applications. These sensors can measure the amounts of specific gases, including oxygen CO2, or VOCs, based on their electrochemical interactions with the CNM electrodes (Thangamani J. et al., 2017). For instance, a sensor array using graphene functionalized with different metal nanoparticles has been created. This "electronic nose" can detect multiple gases simultaneously, differentiating between various VOCs associated with food spoilage (Damdam et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024; Pargoletti and Cappelletti, 2020). Also, a transparent GI using rGO and cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) has been developed. This sensor shows high sensitivity to ethylene, a gas associated with fruit ripening, and can be directly integrated into fruit packaging (Thet Tun et al., 2023; Xiong et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022). Besides, a color-changing GI using CDs has been created to detect ammonia, a common indicator of meat spoilage. This sensor is incorporated into a smart label for meat packaging, providing a clear visual indication of freshness (Alimohammadi et al., 2023; Hsu et al., 2017).

CNMs, particularly CDs, have been explored for developing optical GIs in IntPack applications. These sensors can analyze and measure the concentrations of specific gases by detecting changes in the optical characteristics of CDs, such as fluorescence quenching or wavelength shifts, upon interaction with the target gases (Sun et al., 2023; Wagh et al., 2024). The operation of these optical gas sensors relies on the distinctive optical characteristics of compact discs, which can be altered by the presence of specific gas analytes. Monitoring fluorescence intensity or emission wavelength changes can offer insights into gas concentrations within the packaging environment. These optical GIs can be integrated into packaging materials, labels, or separate devices, providing visual or spectroscopic indications of gas composition, and enabling better monitoring and control of the product's quality and SLife (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2020).

FIs provide valuable information about food quality changes, helping consumers and retailers make informed decisions. GQDs exhibit pH- and VOC-sensitive fluorescence. For example, GQD-embedded films can detect ammonia (meat spoilage) via fluorescence quenching (Bakeshlouy Afshar et al., 2024). Also, studies have shown that GQDs in chitosan/gelatin films reduced microbial growth (Listeria, E. coli) in fruits like avocados (Safitri et al., 2024). Fig. 4 illustrates four methods for preparing pH-responsive FIs: electrospinning, compression molding, solvent casting, and extrusion. Each method has unique characteristics and applications. Electrospinning (Fig. 4a) involves creating polymer nanofibers under a high-voltage electric field, resulting in materials with excellent porosity and uniformity, suitable for food packaging applications. Compression molding (Fig. 4b) uses heat and pressure to produce continuous polymer materials efficiently, making it ideal for large-scale production of bio-based films. Solvent casting (Fig. 4c) is a simpler lab-scale method where polymers are dissolved, cast, and dried, often used with biodegradable materials like polysaccharides and proteins to stabilize natural colorants. Extrusion (Fig. 4d), typically applied to petroleum-based polymers, involves feeding, kneading, and shaping at high temperatures, allowing for fast production of hydrophobic films that can monitor food freshness effectively. These methods vary in complexity, scalability, and the types of materials they accommodate, influencing the stability and monitoring efficacy of Fis (Liu et al., 2022).

A highly sensitive pH sensor using GO has been developed, capable of detecting pH changes as small as 0.1 units. Incorporated into a smart label on packaging, this sensor can predict milk spoilage up to 24 h before traditional sensory evaluation methods (Moustafa et al., 2024; Mukhtar and Kamarolzaman, 2020). In another work, a sensor array using CDs was developed to detect multiple VOCs associated with fish spoilage. This array is integrated into a QR code on the packaging, allowing consumers to scan and obtain real-time freshness information via a smartphone app (Lv et al., 2019). Also, a novel pH-sensitive film has been developed that shows visible color variation in response to fish spoilage while retaining a written pattern (Zhao et al., 2023).

The film in Fig. 5 exhibited visible color changes, indicating that the food had spoiled during the fish freshness detection process, while the written pattern remained intact (Zhai et al., 2018). The schematic illustrates the electrochemical writing mechanism and pH-responsive structural transformations of acylated pelargonidin anthocyanins within gelatin/gellan gum (GGG) composite films. Fig. 5a depicts the electrochemical setup, where a platinum (Pt) needle anode generates localized acidic conditions (pH 2–3) through water electrolysis (4H₂O → 4H⁺ + O₂↑ + 4e⁻), inducing anthocyanins to adopt the orange-red flavylium cation form. Conversely, when connected as a cathode, the Pt needle creates alkaline conditions (pH 10–12) via hydroxyl ion production (4H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ + 2H₂↑), shifting anthocyanins to the yellow anhydrobase anion state. Fig. 5b details the molecular basis of these chromatic transitions: under acidic conditions, the anthocyanins stabilize as flavylium ions (structure 1) with strong absorption at 510 nm, while neutral-to-alkaline environments trigger deprotonation to blue-purple quinonoidal bases (structure 2, pH 7–9) and subsequent hydrolysis to yellow chalcone forms (structure 3, pH ≥10). The direction and magnitude of applied current (−6 to +6 mA) enable precise spatial control over proton/hydroxyl ion flux, facilitating programmable multicolor patterning without exogenous inks. This covalent immobilization of anthocyanins within the biopolymer matrix via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions ensures zero dye migration while maintaining pH sensitivity, critical for food-safe applications. The method’s tunability stems from current-dependent electrolysis intensity, with higher currents amplifying pH extremes and anthocyanin content influencing baseline chromatic properties (e.g., 20 mg/100 mL RRA shifts written patterns from yellow-green to blue-green at −4 mA). This synergistic integration of electrochemistry and natural chromophores establishes a green fabrication platform for IntPack labels that combine stable graphical information with active freshness monitoring capabilities.

Changes in moisture content or humidity within the packaging conditions can impact food quality and SLife of certain food products. CNMs, particularly CNTs and graphene, have been combined with moisture-sensitive dyes or probes to develop MIs. The operating principle of these MIs relies on the interaction between the CNMs and the moisture-sensitive dyes or probes that change color or optical properties due to humidity fluctuations in the packaging (Sefrienda et al., 2022; Tabar et al., 2023).

In the context of fruits and vegetables (F&V) packaging, sensor technology plays a crucial role in monitoring and detecting various metabolic byproducts and potential contaminants. These sensors are capable of identifying specific compounds generated during F&V metabolism, such as oxygen, carbon dioxide, ethylene, and aldehydes. Additionally, they can detect the presence of potential endogenous hazards, including pesticide residues, toxins, and microorganisms. This advanced sensing capability enables more effective quality control and safety measures in F&V packaging systems. The aforementioned sensors yield critical data about the qualitative attributes of F&V, encompassing aspects such as freshness, maturity, overall condition, and safety assurance. An evaluation of the sensing response of the fabricated MIs was conducted across a spectrum of relative humidity (RH) levels, ranging from approximately 11–85 %, within a frequency domain of 100 Hz to 100 kHz. Initially, the Plasticized Polyvinyl Chloride (PPVC)/GO-3 % sensor underwent testing to ascertain optimal operational parameters, owing to its superior characteristics as previously determined. Pure polyvinyl chloride (PVC) exhibited no discernible humidity response under any experimental conditions. The results demonstrated that at a frequency of 1 kHz, the PPVC/GO-3 % sensor's response exhibited an inverse relationship with increasing frequency ( Fig. 6a) and demonstrated sensitivity up to 84.20 % RH, with peak responsiveness observed at 100 Hz. However, 1 kHz was ultimately selected as the optimal frequency due to the observed instability of impedance variations at 100 Hz (Fig. 6b). Consequently, subsequent testing of the remaining sensors was conducted at 1 kHz across the RH range of 11–85 % (Moustafa et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CNMs in other intelligent packaging systems
ce:section.ce:para: CNT-based films/coatings have been developed to incorporate QR codes or other optical markers, enabling consumers to access additional product information by scanning the packaging with their mobile devices (Gaudeul and Krawczyk, 2023). SEM analysis of carbon/polymer composite materials ( Fig. 7) reveals intricate river-like patterns and porous voids on the surface. Unlike distinct pores, porosity trends towards the polymer's cross-section. Increasing activated carbon content leads to surface accumulations, agglomerations, and more voids, as shown in Fig. 7c. These voids facilitate ethylene gas diffusion into the activated carbon pores, forming an interconnected structure that aids gas adsorption (Yurtay et al., 2024).

Interactive packaging systems can incorporate technologies like QR codes, AR, or NFC to offer consumers supplementary information regarding the product, e.g., nutritional information, cooking instructions, or product traceability. CNMs have been explored for developing interactive packaging systems by leveraging their unique optical properties and their ability to form functional coatings or films (Ramezani et al., 2025a; Zou et al., 2016). Table 2 shows recent studies on CNM-based IFP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanotubes for enhancing barrier/mechanical properties of intelligent films
ce:section.ce:para: Recent advancements in CNMs have significantly enhanced the capabilities of IntPack systems, especially in the domains of food safety and quality assurance (Chelliah et al., 2021). This chapter investigates the influence of different CNMs on polysaccharides, proteins, and biopolyesters, focusing on recent case studies that highlight their applications in IntPack.

The incorporation of CNTs into polysaccharide-based materials has enhanced the mechanical and barrier characteristics of the ensuing materials. For example, starch nanocomposites containing MWCNTs and bacterial cellulose nanofibrils showed up to a 52 % increase in TS and a 39 % decrease in WVP. This synergistic effect demonstrates the potential of combining CNTs with other NMs to address multiple packaging requirements simultaneously (Daza-Orsini et al., 2024). GO has been used to enhance the properties of polysaccharide-based films significantly. In chitosan/ carboxymethyl cellulose films, the addition of GO combined with cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) improved TS by 35 % and oxygen barrier properties by 42 %. This highlights the potential of GO to enhance both mechanical and barrier properties concurrently (Chen et al., 2020). Additionally, GO has been incorporated into alginate films to improve their antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, which are crucial for extending SLife and ensuring the safety of food products (Han Lyn et al., 2021; Sabater i Serra et al., 2020). CDs have been incorporated into polysaccharide matrices to impart additional functionalities. For example, CDs in alginate films have shown enhanced antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, which are essential for extending SLife of perishable foods. Furthermore, CDs combined with CNCs in casein protein films have enhanced UV-shielding abilities, addressing the challenge of light-sensitive food products (Botalo et al., 2024; Santos and Martins, 2024).

CNTs have been used to enhance the mechanical and barrier properties of protein-based films. Zein films reinforced with MWCNTs and cellulose nanofibrils showed a 39 % increase in TS and a 51 % improvement in oxygen barrier properties. This significant enhancement in oxygen barrier is particularly relevant for preserving oxygen-sensitive foods (Khachatryan et al., 2023; Shah et al., 2023). GO has been used to develop protein-based films with improved properties. For instance, GO and lignin nanoparticles enhanced TS by 33 % in whey protein isolate films and reduced oxygen permeability by 46 % in egg white protein films. This combination showcases the potential for developing multifunctional packaging materials. Additionally, GO-reinforced whey hydrogels have been characterized as eco-friendly absorbents for food packaging, demonstrating improved barrier properties in the UV range (Gomide et al., 2022; Purcea Lopes et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2021).

Cyclodextrins have been incorporated into protein-based films to enhance their physical and functional properties. For instance, CDs integrated into gelatin/chitosan bio-nanocomposite films have exhibited enhanced mechanical characteristics and antimicrobial capabilities, rendering them suitable for packaging perishable food products. Additionally, CDs in PVA composites have demonstrated strong fluorescence, UV-blocking abilities, and antimicrobial properties, making them well-suited for developing edible coatings and packaging materials for the food industry (Wang et al., 2023; Wen et al., 2023).

CNTs have been embedded into biopolyester-based matrices to improve their performance. For example, PLA films reinforced with MWCNTs and CNCs showed a 49 % increase in TS and a 58 % reduction in oxygen permeability. This significant enhancement in PLA films is particularly noteworthy given PLA's growing importance as a biodegradable packaging material (Younus et al., 2023). GO has been used to develop biopolyester-based nanocomposites with improved properties. For instance, polybutylene succinate (PBS) nanofiber membranes mixed with GO had higher efficiency in protein capture, demonstrating the potential for developing IntPack materials with enhanced functionalities. Additionally, GO has been incorporated into biodegradable food packaging to improve resistance to bacterial attack and enhance thermal and light stability (Alves et al., 2022; Sathirapongsasuti et al., 2023; Shekhar et al., 2023). Biopolyester-based films have been enhanced through the incorporation of CDs. For example, CDs in PVA composites have shown strong fluorescence, UV-blocking activity, and antimicrobial properties, making them suitable for developing IntPack materials that enable real-time monitoring of food quality and safety. Furthermore, CDs have been synthesized from various carbon sources to investigate their potential uses in food packaging, including antimicrobial and antioxidant properties (Alaş et al., 2022; Gedda et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of CNM-based intelligent packaging for different food products

ce:section.ce:para: Seafood products are highly perishable and require innovative packaging solutions to retain their safety and quality. A recent study reviewed various packaging technologies, including IntPack with FIs and TTIs, to extend SLife of seafood products. The study discussed using CNMs to create sensors that can track seafood quality and provide updates to consumers and stores in real-time (Kontominas et al., 2021).

Fig. 8 illustrates the development and application of carrageenan-based active and intelligent films designed to preserve the quality of protein-rich products through real-time freshness monitoring. The experiment involved wrapping shrimp samples with these films and storing them at 4°C for 15 days. To evaluate spoilage and visual changes in both the shrimp and the films, researchers measured pH, color, and Total Viable Count (TVC) of bacteria at 5-day intervals, as shown in Fig. 8a-d. The results demonstrated that shrimp wrapped in carrageenan-only film exhibited red discoloration by day 5, indicating the onset of spoilage. In contrast, shrimp samples wrapped with Car/SPA/Ti-CD1 % and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3 % films maintained their original color for up to 5 days, suggesting these films effectively delayed the spoilage process. Discoloration of the Car/SPA/Ti-CD1 % and Car/SPA/Ti-CD3 % films began to appear after 10 days, signaling the onset of shrimp decay. Among the tested films, the Car/SPA/Ti-CD3 % formulation demonstrated superior preservation performance (Riahi et al., 2024).

The meat industry has also seen significant advancements in IntPack technologies. Recent innovations include the advancement of IntPack monitoring systems to track the freshness, microbiological status, and SLife of meat products. CNMs have been utilized to create GIs that detect specific gases and VOCs associated with meat spoilage, providing valuable information about product quality (Andre et al., 2021).

In F&V packaging, GIs can detect specific substances produced during the metabolic processes of F&V, such as ethylene, oxygen, and CO2. CNMs have been used to develop GIs that monitor these gases, helping to ensure the freshness and ripeness of the products (Fan et al., 2021). The effective combination of polyaniline (PANI) and electrically conductive graphene in rGO-PANI nanosheets creates highly sensitive gas sensing materials. To showcase their performance, rGO-PANI composites were applied to a ceramic tube, and their resistance changes were monitored in different gas environments (Chang et al., 2021).

Using CNMs in packaging provides many advantages, but it's important to consider the potential toxicity and environmental issues that could arise from their use in food packaging (Basiuk et al., 2011; Kotsilkov et al., 2018). Addressing toxicity concerns and environmental impacts is critical to ensuring the responsible use of CNMs in food packaging.

A key concern about using CNMs in food packaging is their potential toxicity and health effects on humans. Although CNMs are composed of carbon, their nanoscale dimensions and unique properties may raise safety concerns (Baranwal et al., 2018). Several studies have investigated the potential toxicity of CNMs, focusing on their interactions with biological systems and potential adverse effects. These studies have explored various aspects, including cytotoxicity, genotoxicity, and potential for inflammatory responses (Jović et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2020; Raja et al., 2019). While some studies have reported low or negligible toxicity for certain CNMs, others have highlighted potential adverse effects under specific conditions or exposure scenarios. The CNM type, their surface chemistry, and the presence of impurities or contaminants can influence their toxicological profile (Asakura et al., 2010; Baysal et al., 2018). Comprehensive toxicological assessments and compliance with established guidelines and regulations are essential for ensuring the safe use of CNMs in food packaging. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) and the U.S. FDA, both government agencies, have established processes to evaluate the safety of NMs used in food packaging (Onyeaka et al., 2022; Störmer et al., 2024).

In addition to potential human health concerns, the environmental impact of CNMs should also be considered. While CNMs are composed of carbon, their production processes and potential release into the environment may have implications for ecosystems and environmental sustainability. The manufacture of CNMs frequently utilizes energy-intensive techniques, including CVD and arc discharge, which can produce waste or byproducts. It is essential to ensure that these processes are optimized to minimize environmental impacts and comply with relevant environmental regulations (Jiang et al., 2020; Simeonova et al., 2007). Furthermore, the potential environmental release of CNMs through the disposal or degradation of packaging materials should be evaluated. While some CNMs, such as graphene and CNTs, are generally considered stable and non-biodegradable, their nanoscale dimensions and unique properties may influence their behavior and interactions within environmental systems (Gupta et al., 2018). Life Cycle Assessments (LCAs) and environmental risk assessments should be conducted to understand the potential environmental impacts of CNM-based packaging materials over their full life cycle, from manufacture to end-of-life disposal or recycling (Bainbridge et al., 2023; Lenz e Silva et al., 2020).

National and international authorities have established regulatory frameworks and guidelines that govern the use of CNMs in food packaging. The regulations are intended to guarantee the safety of materials that come into contact with food and safeguard consumer well-being (Thapliyal et al., 2024). In the European Union, the use of NMs in food packaging and containers is regulated by the Framework Regulation No 1935/2004 and specific regulations, such as Regulation No 10/2011 for plastic materials intended to contact with food. These regulations require the evaluation of NMs for their safety and potential migration into food (Carvalho et al., 2022; Silano et al., 2020). The U.S. FDA oversees the regulation of materials that come into contact with food, including NMs, under federal law. The FDA has published guidance and recommendations for assessing the safety of NMs used in food contact applications (Olawore et al., 2024). Many other countries and regions, including Canada, Australia, and Japan, have also developed regulations and guidelines for the use of NMs in food packaging (Deepika Rani et al., 2018; Gergely et al., 2017). Manufacturers and researchers must comply with the relevant regulations and guidelines when developing and using CNM-based packaging materials for food applications. Proper safety assessments, migration studies, and regulatory approvals may be required before commercialization and widespread use of these materials.

Although there have been some reductions, the high production costs of CNMs still present a challenge for their widespread use in the food packaging industry. Further cost reductions are necessary to make these materials economically viable on a large scale (Jafarzadeh et al., 2023). Also, developing scalable manufacturing processes for CNM-based sensors and their integration into packaging materials is crucial. Current production techniques, like CVD, are energy-intensive and not easily scalable (Nufer and Brunton Alan Dalton, 2019). Besides, there is a need for industry standards to ensure consistency in the performance and interpretation of sensor data across different products and manufacturers. Establishing these standards is essential for the reliable use of CNM-based IntPack (Liu and Gao, 2017; Siddiqui et al., 2022). Educating consumers about the benefits and safety of CNM-based IntPack systems is necessary to build trust and acceptance. Awareness campaigns may be required to help consumers understand and trust the information provided by these systems (Francis et al., 2024). Besides, comprehensive safety assessments and the development of appropriate regulatory frameworks are essential for the widespread adoption of CNM-based packaging technologies. Regulators should develop clear rules for assessing the safety of these materials in food packaging (Ramezani et al., 2025b). On the other hand, potential toxicity concerns and environmental impacts associated with CNMs must be carefully considered. Assessing the toxicity and environmental risks is crucial to using these materials safely and responsibly (Coccini et al., 2013; Rozhin et al., 2022).

Combining CNMs with other NMs can develop multifunctional packaging materials. Creating hybrid systems that integrate multiple intelligent functionalities, such as TTIs, GIs, and FIs, can address various packaging requirements simultaneously (Cvek et al., 2022; Kumari et al., 2024). Also, further development of CNM-based GIs for gas detection and fluorescent sensors in monitoring various parameters is needed. Real-time monitoring of the supply chain using IoT-enabled sensors can enhance food safety and quality control (Singh et al., 2019; Tanguy, 2019). Advancing eco-friendly synthesis methods, particularly for CDs using biomass and food waste as precursors, is essential. Developing effective recycling strategies and enhancing the biodegradability of CNM-based packaging materials can address environmental concerns (Abbas et al., 2022). Employing computational modeling can yield valuable insights into the characteristics and performance of CNM-based packaging materials, thereby enabling more effective material design and optimization. Simulation approaches can further assist in elucidating the interactions between CNMs and other packaging constituents (Comer et al., 2015; Zeng et al., 2015).

By addressing these future directions, the food industry can realize the full potential of CNMs in developing innovative IntPack solutions. These advancements will enhance food quality, safety, and sustainability while addressing challenges associated with the commercial adoption and implementation of CNM-based packaging materials.

The application of CNMs in IFP represents a major improvement in addressing the challenges of food safety, quality, and sustainability. This review has examined the various types and characteristics of CNMs, including CNTs, graphene, fullerenes, and CDs, and their potential to improve the barrier and mechanical performance of packaging materials. The integration of CNMs into polymer matrices has been shown to significantly improve gas and moisture barrier properties, as well as mechanical strength, stiffness, and toughness, thereby extending SLife of food products. The remarkable properties of CNMs, including high electrical conductivity, thermal stability, and adjustable optical and electronic features, have facilitated the creation of innovative integrated packaging systems. These systems include TTIs, GIs, FIs, and controlled-release systems for antimicrobials or antioxidants, which provide real-time data on the product's condition and improve its protection throughout the supply chain. Despite the promising benefits, the adoption of CNM-based IntPack faces several challenges. These include the high production costs of CNMs, scalability issues, the need for standardization, consumer acceptance, regulatory approval, and potential toxicity and environmental impacts. Comprehensive toxicological studies, environmental risk analyses, and adherence to regulations are necessary to enable the safe and responsible application of CNM-based packaging materials.

Looking ahead, several areas of research and development hold promise for further advancing the application of CNMs in IntPack. These include: (i) Developing scalable and energy-efficient production techniques to replace current methods like CVD or arc discharge is crucial for commercial adoption; (ii) Combining CNMs with other NMs to create hybrid systems that integrate multiple intelligent functionalities, such as TTIs, GIs, and FIs; (iii) Further development of CNM-based GIs for gas detection and fluorescent sensors for monitoring various parameters, and integrating these sensors with IoT technologies for real-time monitoring throughout the supply chain; (iv) Advancing eco-friendly synthesis methods, particularly for CDs using biomass and food waste as precursors, and developing effective recycling strategies and enhancing the biodegradability of CNM-based packaging materials; (v) Conducting comprehensive toxicological and environmental impact studies, developing standardized testing protocols, and achieving regulatory harmonization across regions to facilitate the approval and adoption of these technologies; (vi) Computational modeling to understand CNM-based packaging materials, enabling more efficient material design and optimization; (vii) Addressing challenges in scaling up production and industrial implementation, developing cost-effective manufacturing processes, quality control measures, and integrating CNM-based packaging materials into existing supply chains.

By leveraging the capabilities of CNMs, the food industry can develop innovative integrated packaging solutions that enhance food quality, safety, and sustainability. Addressing the challenges associated with the adoption and implementation of CNM-based packaging will require collaborative efforts from researchers, industry stakeholders, and regulatory authorities. This coordinated approach is crucial to realizing the transformative potential of CNMs in revolutionizing food packaging, ultimately contributing to a more efficient and sustainable food supply chain that benefits consumers, producers, and the environment.

Ghazaleh Ramezani: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Elham Assadpour: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Wanli Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Seid Mahdi Jafari: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.
